1: A new divine speech formula marking the beginning of a distinct subsection. Lev 6:1-7 (English; Hebrew 5:20-26) closes the asham laws by addressing interpersonal fraud and theft — and classifying these as covenant offences against YHWH himself. The section demonstrates that the horizontal and vertical dimensions of sin are inseparable: cheating a neighbor is simultaneously trespassing against YHWH.
2: nefesh ki techeta uma'ala ma'al baYHWH (a soul who sins and commits a trespass against YHWH): the key interpretive move of this section: interpersonal fraud — lying about a deposit, robbery, deception of a neighbor — is categorized as ma'al baYHWH (trespass against YHWH). The horizontal wrong (against the neighbor) is simultaneously a vertical wrong (against YHWH). piqqadon (deposit, entrustment): a common commercial arrangement in the ancient Near East, governed by its own legal codes (Hammurabi §§120-126). The Levitical law classifies the violation of such trust as sacred encroachment, not merely civil wrong.
3: o nefesh asher yishava al shaqer (or a person who swears falsely): the false oath is the climactic case — it invokes YHWH's name in support of a lie, compounding the interpersonal fraud with blasphemy. The categories in vv. 2-3 (deposit fraud, robbery, extortion, lost-property fraud, false oath) are unified by their common structure: using speech or relationship to take what belongs to another. This is the sin of deception in economic relationships — a violation of the ninth commandment (‘do not bear false witness’) applied to commercial life.
4: vehayah ki yecheta ve-ashem (when he has sinned and is guilty): the causal vehayah ki (‘and it shall be when’) marks the consequence. veheshiv et hagazel asher gazal (he shall return what he took by robbery): the verb shub (Hiphil: heshiv) = ‘restore, return, make restitution.’ The atonement process for interpersonal fraud requires first returning the stolen goods — confession and sacrifice without restitution is incomplete. This is the OT foundation for the NT principle that reconciliation with the offended party precedes valid worship: ‘if you are offering your gift at the altar and there remember that your brother has something against you, first go and be reconciled’ (Matt 5:23-24).
5: al kol asher yishava alav lashaqer veshallem oto verosho yosif alav chamishitav (for anything about which he swore falsely — he shall make full restitution, adding one-fifth of it): the 20% surcharge (chamishit) is the same penalty as for misappropriation of sacred property (5:16). The theological implication: a neighbor's property, protected by oath, has the same sacred status as temple property. beyom ashmatoh (on the day of his guilt offering): the restitution precedes or accompanies the sacrifice — not a deferrable obligation but an immediate one.
6: ve-et ashamo yavi laYHWH ayil tamim (he shall bring his guilt offering to YHWH — a flawless ram): after restitution comes sacrifice. The order is significant: economic justice first, then ritual atonement. The asham ram for interpersonal fraud is identical to the asham for violating sacred things (5:15) — confirming that the neighbor's property is as sacred as temple property in YHWH's sight.
7: venikhpar lav lifnei YHWH (atonement is made for him before YHWH): the atonement formula closing the asham section. vekhol asher ya'aseh le'echad mekulam asher yit'ashma bah (for anything he may have done to incur guilt): the comprehensive scope — the asham covers the full range of covenant violations enumerated in vv. 2-5. The forgiveness formula confirms: the combination of restitution + sacrifice provides complete resolution of both the horizontal wrong (the neighbor is made whole) and the vertical wrong (YHWH's covenant is repaired).
8: Another divine speech formula: vayedaber YHWH el Moshe lemor. The section vv. 8-13 begins the ‘priestly torah’ or ‘instructions for the offerings’ — a different literary genre from the preceding ‘case law’ format of chs 4-6:7. Lev 6:8-7:38 provides the officiants' manual for handling each sacrifice, including the priestly portions. The divine speech marking a new section also signals a change in addressee: previously addressed to the people (via Moses), now addressed to Aaron and the priests.
9: zot torat ha-olah (this is the Torah of the burnt offering): the torat formula introduces each major section of the priestly manual (cf. 6:14, 25; 7:1, 11). hi ha-olah al moqtah al hamizbeach kol halailah (the burnt offering remains on its hearth on the altar all night): the olah is unique among offerings in being entirely consumed on the altar — no portion for priests or offerer. Its all-night burning distinguishes it: the fire continues even when the worshiping community is absent, asserting the continuous character of Israel's consecration to YHWH. veha'eish hamizbeach tuqad bo (the fire of the altar shall be kept burning on it): the perpetual fire is the subject of the next unit.
10: velabash hakohen middo bad (the priest puts on his linen garment): the linen (bad) priestly garments — white, undyed, non-wool — are the standard dress for the daily service. venas et hadeshan el mitz ad lamizbeach (he takes up the ashes left from the burnt offering on the altar and places them beside the altar): the removal of ashes is a ritual act requiring the priestly vestments. Even the disposal of what remains after the sacrifice is governed by holiness protocols — nothing about YHWH's altar is treated as ordinary refuse.
11: ufashat et begadav velabash begadim acherim (he shall take off his garments and put on other clothes): a change of garments for the ash-removal journey. The holy vestments are worn in the sanctuary; ordinary clothes are worn for the ash disposal outside the camp. The same principle of contextual holiness — different sacred protocols for different spaces — governs the movement between the altar (holy) and the ash-dump (clean but not holy).
12: veha'eish al hamizbeach tuqad bo (the fire on the altar must be kept burning): repeated from v.9 to frame vv. 10-11 as an interruption. uveer alav hakohen etzim baboqer baboqer (the priest shall add wood to it every morning): the daily wood-feeding is the priest's first morning act. The fire that began at the altar's inauguration (Lev 9:24: fire from before YHWH) must never be allowed to go out — it is the sign of YHWH's ongoing acceptance and presence.
13: eish tamid tuqad al hamizbeach lo tichbeh (a continual fire shall be kept burning on the altar; it shall not go out): the tamid principle (continual, perpetual) is fundamental to Levitical worship. Multiple elements of the tabernacle/temple service are tamid: the fire (6:13), the lampstand (Exod 27:20), the bread of the Presence (Exod 25:30), the incense (Exod 30:8). The tamid offerings frame Israel's life in perpetual liturgy. Revelation 4:8 (‘day and night they never cease to say, Holy, holy, holy’) is the new covenant's eternal tamid — continuous worship before YHWH.
14: vezot torat haminchah (this is the Torah of the grain offering): the minchah section begins. bene Aharon yaqrivu otah lifnei YHWH el penei hamizbeach (Aaron's sons shall offer it before YHWH, in front of the altar): the grain offering instructions are now addressed to the officiants. The grain offering (minchah, gift/tribute) consists of fine flour, oil, and salt; it accompanies other offerings and is sometimes made as a standalone gift. It represents the offerer's produce — the agricultural domain consecrated to YHWH.
15: veheram mimenu bequmtzo misolet haminchah umishamnah (a priest takes a handful from the grain offering — from the flour and oil): the kometz procedure for the minchah: a fistful is taken and burned as the azkarah (memorial portion). al kol halevonah asher al haminchah (and all the frankincense that is on the grain offering): all the frankincense is burned even though only a handful of flour. The azkarah rises before YHWH as reyach nichoach (pleasing aroma) — the ascending smoke is the ‘memorial’ portion that keeps the offerer in YHWH's remembrance.
16: vehannoteret mimenu yochaluhu Aharon uvanav (what remains Aaron and his sons eat): the remainder of the grain offering is priestly food. matzot te'achel (it shall be eaten without leaven/yeast): unleavened bread is required in the holy precinct. The absence of leaven in the priestly food connects to Passover theology — leaven represents the old, corrupted life; unleavened bread is the food of the redeemed, the holy, the new (cf. Paul in 1 Cor 5:7-8: ‘let us celebrate the festival... with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth’).
17: lo te'afeh chametz (it shall not be baked with leaven): repeated and emphatic. chelqam natati otah me'ishai (I have given it as their portion from my food offerings): the priestly portion is explicitly YHWH's gift to the priests — ‘my food offerings’ means the offerings belong to YHWH first; he then grants the priests their portion. The same principle governs the NT ministry: those who serve the altar share in the altar's provisions (1 Cor 9:13-14). kodesh kodashim hi kachatat uchaasham (it is most holy, like the sin offering and the guilt offering): the grain offering's remainder is classified as kodesh kodashim (most holy) — the highest sanctity grade, which restricts consumption to the sanctuary precincts and to male priests only.
18: kol zachar bibne Aharon yochalenu (every male among Aaron's sons may eat it): the gender restriction on kodesh kodashim consumption reflects the priestly grading system: holiest things require maximum qualification (full-status male priests only). chuqat olam ledoroteichem (a permanent statute throughout your generations): the chuqat olam formula marks permanent, unchangeable ordinances. kol asher yiga bahem yiqdash (whoever touches them becomes holy): ‘contagious holiness’ — contact with the most holy things conveys holiness. This principle is the inverse of the impurity-transfer principle: as uncleanness transfers by contact, so does holiness. Christ's ‘contagious holiness’ is the NT expression: touching him heals and cleanses (Mark 1:41; Luke 8:46).
19: Another divine speech formula: the ordination offering section (vv. 19-23) follows. This is the daily grain offering of the high priest — not a one-time event but the permanent daily practice instituted at ordination.
20: zeh qorban Aharon uvanov (this is the offering of Aaron and his sons): the high priest's personal offering is distinct from his mediatorial offerings for the people. asirit ha-ephah solet minchah tamid (a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a regular grain offering): the same amount as the poorest person's offering (5:11) — the high priest offers what the destitute person offers! This theological leveling communicates that the high priest himself depends on the mercy of atonement; he is not above the system he administers. chatzi baboqer vechatzi ba'arev (half in the morning and half in the evening): the twice-daily framing of the priestly day. The tamid sacrifice (Num 28:3-8) structures Israel's daily worship at morning and evening.
21: al machabat bashemen te'aseh (it shall be prepared on a griddle with oil): the ordination offering is a cooked grain offering. murbeket tavienah (bring it well mixed/soaked): the preparation method ensures a specific texture. The detail of the high priest's daily offering is provided with precision — nothing in the priestly service is improvised or left to preference. The exactness of YHWH's instructions models the exactness of Christ's obedience: not approximately what the Father required, but precisely (Phil 2:8: ‘obedient to death, even death on a cross’).
22: vehakohen hamashiach tachtav mibanov ya'aseh otah (the anointed priest who succeeds him among his sons shall offer it): the ordination offering is inherited — the successor priest continues the same daily offering pattern. The continuity of the anointed priest's daily offering models the continuity of Israel's priestly ministry. khalil le-YHWH tuktar (it shall be entirely burned to YHWH): the high priest's grain offering is entirely consumed — no portion is eaten. The same logic as the burnt offering: the officiant who intercedes for others offers himself entirely, retaining nothing for himself.
23: vekol minchah lakohen kalil tihyeh lo te'achel (every grain offering from a priest shall be entirely burned; it shall not be eaten): the general rule confirmed: priests' own grain offerings are entirely YHWH's. When priests eat the people's grain offerings (v.16), that is the appointed portion from YHWH; but priests cannot eat their own offerings, which belong entirely to God. The self-offering of the priest is total and unreserved.
24: A fourth divine speech formula in this chapter, introducing the sin offering instructions for priests (vv. 24-30). The shift to direct address to Aaron and the priests continues.
25: zot torat hachatat (this is the Torah of the sin offering): the priestly manual for the chattat. bimqom asher yishachet ha-olah tishachet hachatat (in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, the sin offering shall be slaughtered): the slaughter location of the chattat is the same as the olah — the north side of the altar (Lev 1:11). The spatial alignment communicates the equivalence of the two: both are equally legitimate, equally holy, at the same place before YHWH. qodesh qodashim hi (it is most holy): the sin offering meat is classified as kodesh kodashim — the highest holiness grade.
26: hakohen hamechaper otah yochalenah (the priest who offers it as a sin offering shall eat it): the priest who performs the atonement rite eats the sin offering's meat. This is the economic provision for the Levitical priesthood but also has theological significance: the priest who handles the sin offering participates in it. bimaqom qadosh te'achel bechatzar ohel mo'ed (in a holy place it shall be eaten, in the court of the tent of meeting): the location restriction confines the sin-offering meal to the sanctuary precinct. The sanctity of the meat requires a holy context for consumption.
27: kol asher yiga bivisarah yiqdash (whoever touches its flesh becomes holy): ‘contagious holiness’ extended to the sin offering's flesh. The sin offering — which carries the sin of the offerer through the priest's act — is itself kodesh kodashim and communicates holiness to whatever touches it. The paradox: the vessel of sin-bearing becomes the most holy object. Christ as the sin-bearer (‘him who knew no sin to be sin for us,’ 2 Cor 5:21) is the ultimate ‘most holy’ thing — and contact with him through faith sanctifies (Heb 2:11: ‘the one who sanctifies and those who are sanctified are all of one family’).
28: ukhli cheres asher tevushal bo yishaber (the clay pot in which it was cooked must be broken): clay is porous and absorbs the fluids of whatever is cooked in it. A clay pot that has absorbed sin-offering residue must be destroyed — the absorbed holiness cannot be fully removed from the absorbent material. keli nechoshet (a bronze pot): bronze, being non-porous and scourable, can be cleaned. The distinction between porous (clay) and non-porous (bronze) materials in relation to absorbed sanctity illustrates the concrete, physical theology of the Levitical system — holiness has real material implications.
29: kol zachar bakohanim yochal otah qodesh qodashim hi (every male among the priests may eat it; it is most holy): the sin offering's meat is restricted to male priests in the sanctuary. The graduated access of the sacrificial system mirrors the graduated approach to YHWH: the holy of holies (high priest only once per year), the holy place (priests only), the court (all Israel), outside the court (Gentiles, excluded). The NT dissolution of these gradations is the theological argument of Hebrews and Ephesians: in Christ, the full access of the most holy is opened to all (Heb 10:19-22; Eph 2:18).
30: vekol chatat asher yuva midamah el ohel mo'ed lekapper bakodesh lo te'achel ba'eish tisaref (no sin offering whose blood is brought into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the holy place shall be eaten; it shall be burned with fire): the key rule distinguishing ordinary sin offerings from the special ones. When the sin offering's blood is taken inside the veil (as in Yom Kippur, Lev 16, and the offerings for priest and congregation, Lev 4:3-21), the entire carcass must be burned outside the camp — it cannot be eaten. The reason: the blood has entered the most holy place carrying the sin burden; the associated flesh is too charged for ordinary consumption. Hebrews 13:11-12 applies this: Jesus suffered ‘outside the gate’ as the fulfillment of the burned sin-offering whose blood entered the most holy place.
